coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots from corn powder and their application in reduce charge recombination and increase free charge carriers
coredata.dc:creator: Teymourinia, Hakimeh
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
Amiri, Omid
Safardoust-Hojaghan, Hossein
coredata.dc:description: GQDs have been synthesized by using a novel and green precursor (corn powder). Furthermore, GQDs/TiO2 composite has been prepared by using graphene quantum dots as capping agent. Incorporation of graphene quantum dots in TiO2 nanoparticles reduce charge recombination, extend the absorption spectrum of TiO2 to the visible region, and increase the accessible surface area of TiO2 nanoparticles. Therefore, the significant enhancement of photocatalytic activity for degrading the Rhodamin B has been achieved. Characterization of the samples was carried out by Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and photoluminescence (PL).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Charge recombination
Nanocomposite
Photocatalysis
Nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In situ synthesis of graphene quantum dots/TiO2 nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of a) corn powder and GQDs and b) TiO2 and TiO2/GQD nanocomposite.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles for a) the corn powder, GQD, b) TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 composite.
The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of as-prepared a) GQDs, b) TiO2 and c) GQDs/TiO2.
TEM images of graphene quantum dots (a and b) and GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites (c and d).
a) PL, Uv–Vis and PLE results of GQDs, b) the ultraviolet–visible (UV/Vis) absorption spectrum and c) Pl of GQDs, TiO2, and TiO2/GQD.
Schematic of blue shift mechanism at PL of GQDs/TiO2 composite.
As-synthesized graphene quantum dots under normal light (a) and UV light (b, c, and d).
Effect of different parameters such as temperature and time on PL of as-synthesized GQDs.
Photodegradation results under UV lights for GQDs, TiO2, and TiO2/GQD.
Possible mechanism for degradation of RhB by TiO2/GQD nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots from corn powder and their application in reduce charge recombination and increase free charge carriers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317-51167, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: GQDs have been synthesized by using a novel and green precursor (corn powder). Furthermore, GQDs/TiO2 composite has been prepared by using graphene quantum dots as capping agent. Incorporation of graphene quantum dots in TiO2 nanoparticles reduce charge recombination, extend the absorption spectrum of TiO2 to the visible region, and increase the accessible surface area of TiO2 nanoparticles. Therefore, the significant enhancement of photocatalytic activity for degrading the Rhodamin B has been achieved. Characterization of the samples was carried out by Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and photoluminescence (PL).

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays huge budget are spend to treat waste water of factory and drinking water. Most of current method still are not enough efficient, especially in developing country, and cause serious environmental problem. Every year 6 to 8 million people die annually from the consequences of disasters and water-related diseases. In this regards, heterogeneous photocatalysts have been taking great attention for the removal of organic dyes and pathogens [1,2].

Among various semiconductor photocatalysts, titanium dioxide is intensively investigated as the most promising photocatalyst because of its biological and chemical inertness, low cost, non-toxicity, strong oxidizing power, and long-term stability [3–7]. Wide band gap of TiO2 and its high recombination rate limited its degradation efficiency. Until the present time, many efforts have been done to solve these problems such as using noble metals, carbon nanotube and so on [8–10]. These were used for light harvesting and expanding the light absorption range or suppressing the electron-hole recombination [9]. Kamat et al. report 10–15% more degradation compare to the bare TiO2 by using noble metals [10]. They also reported that degradation rate was enhanced by loading noble metal on TiO2 surface. Wolfgang Sigmund and et al. combine TiO2 and carbon nanotubes (CNT) which show better photocatalytic activity compare to the bare TiO2 [9]. They proposed two possible mechanism to explain the enhancement of the photocatalytic properties of CNT–TiO2 composites: first, CNT acts as an acceptor compound and exited electron of TiO2 was transferred to CNT and reduced the charge recombination. Second, CNT works as sensitizer and transfer electron to the titanium dioxide [9].

Herein, we used carbon dots to reduce the recombination rate and improve light harvesting of TiO2 which lead to improve the photocatalysis efficiency of TiO2. Carbon quantum dots are small carbon nanoparticles with the size less than 10nm that have been the current focus in research because of its unique properties, the high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, environmental friendship. Since 2004 that X.Y. Xu have prepared the carbon dots by electrophoresis for the first time [11], many other synthetic methods including electrochemical synthesis, hydrothermal synthesis, acidic oxidation and microwave have been studied [12–15].

Properties of carbon dots such as optical properties strongly depend on preparation method and precursors. In this research, we prepared graphene quantum dots from corn powder as green precursor. In addition, TiO2/graphene quantum dots composite was prepared with in situ method. Then its photocatalysis activity was evaluated by degradation of Rhodamine B as organic pollution.

In typical synthesis, 0.25g of corn powder was dispersed into 25mL DI water and stirred for 30min. Then the solution was transferred into a Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180°C for 8h. The final product was soluble in water. The solution was centrifuged at 14000 for 15min to separate the deposit, the brown solution was graphene quantum dots (GQDs). GC-2550TG (Teif Gostar Faraz Company, Iran) were used for all chemical analyses.

TiO2 nanoparticles was prepared according to the reference [16] with some modification. Briefly, 2ml Tetraethyl Orthotitanate (TEOT) was dissolved in 5ml ethanol. Then the above solution was injected drop wise into the solution of 0.2mL acetic acid and 50mL DI water under ultrasonic irradiation for 2h. The obtained products were dried at 40°C for 48h. Final product was a white powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ synthesis of graphene quantum dots/TiO2 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs/TiO2 composite was prepared by in situ method. In a typical synthesis, 2ml Tetraethyl Orthotitanate (TEOT) was dissolved in 5ml ethanol. Then the above solution was injected drop wise into the solution of 0.2mL acetic acid and 50mL DI water and GQD solution (obtained in part 2.1) under ultrasonic irradiation for 2h. The obtained products were dried at 40°C for 48h. Final product was a brownish powder.

The photocatalytic decomposition of the Rhodamin B was carried out in a beaker containing a suspension of 1mg/mL of GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite or TiO2 nanoparticles which were added in 50mL RhB (10mg/L) solution under UV light irradiation. Before the irradiation, the suspension was magnetically stirred in the dark for 30min to ensure the adsorption/desorption equilibrium. During the irradiation, 4mL solution is sampled and centrifuged to remove the catalysts. The filtrates were analyzed by recording the variations of the absorption band maximum (554nm).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis was used to understand the components and structures of the products and investigating surface functional groups of obtained GQDs and their interaction with TiO2 nanoparticles. Fig. 1 illustrated the FTIR spectra of the corn powder and the GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1a, in corn powder, absorption bands at 3317, 2926 and 1647 are imputed to the stretching vibration of OH, CH and CC, respectively. The weak peak at 1720 and 2850cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration aldehyde group. The absorption bands at 1537 and 1338cm−1 can be ascribed to the nitro group. Similarly, in the FTIR spectrum of GQD the broad peaks at 3100–3500cm−1 is due to OH and NH. The peaks at 1725 and 1665cm−1 demonstrated carboxyl and amid groups. In addition, the peaks at 1615 and 1450cm−1 can be result of skeletal vibration of aromatic rings of GQDs. In both spectra, the absorption bands at 1154 and 1199cm−1 in corn powder and GQD, respectively affiliated phosphate group because of pyridoxal phosphate (vitamin B6) in Corn.

Fig. 1b shows FT-IR spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/GQD nanocomposite. There is a sharp peak at 1423cm−1 that can be relevant to the lattice vibrations of TiO2. The absorption bands around 3400 and 1627cm−1 which attributed to OH stretching vibration, and OH bending of H2O molecules that absorbed onto the surface. In these diagrams existence of light peak at ~1012cm−1 is due to characteristic OO stretching vibration. It should be noted, the broad peak around 400–900cm−1 in TiO2 nanoparticles (due to Ti-O-Ti bond) shifted to higher wavenumber (due to a combination effect of Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C bond) in GQDs/TiO2nanocomposite, substantiating the formation of Ti-O-C bond. In addition to, in FT-IR spectra of TiO2/GQD nanocomposite peaks at 1650 and 3100–3500cm−1 can be attributed to the CC, OH and NH group, respectively. On the other side, in this spectra, it can be observed that absorption bands at 1720, 1527 and 1329cm−1 are corresponded to the CO, NH and CN, respectively, which demonstrated the presence of amide and carboxyl group on the surface of the GQDs in synthesized composites. On the other side, the absorption band at 1154cm−1 corresponded to phosphate groups of GQD.

Fig. 2 shows typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles for the Corn powder, GQD, TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 composite. The XRD patterns of GQDs shows a broad peak at around 24Å corresponding to the (002) peak which is matched with JCPDS Card No, 75-0444 and the Corn powder XRD pattern shows a broad peak that is result of its amorphous structure. As shown in Fig. 2b, XRD pattern of GQDs/TiO2composite exhibited the same characteristic diffraction peaks as TiO2 nanoparticles which all diffraction peaks can be well indexed to pure anatase structural titanium oxide while no peaks of the rutile or brookite phase were detected, indicating satisfactory purity of these products. The existing peaks at 2θ values of 25.68°, 38.18°, 47.89°, 54.41°, 62.84°, 69.41° and 75.27° corresponding to the crystal planes of (101), (004), (200), (105), (204),(116) and (215) respectively point to the formation of anatase titanium dioxide that indexed to JCPDS Card No. 04–0477. No characteristic diffraction peak of GQD is detected in GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite because of low amount, low crystallinity and high dispersion of doped GQDs. It should be noted that the average crystallite size of anatase obtained from the peaks by the Scherrer equation (L=0.89λ/β cosθ), that have average size between 2.4 and 3.2nm. Fig. 3a, b and c show the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of as-prepared GQDs, TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2, respectively. The GQDs is consisted of regularly shaped particles with average size between 20 and 40nm (Fig. 3a). The TiO2 nanoparticles in Fig. 3b have relatively uniform particles in large lumps with a wide size range (40–100nm), due to growth and agglomeration. The GQDs/TiO2nanocomposites (Fig. 3c) formed in irregularly shapes and non-uniform particles with average size between 20 and 60nm.

Fig. 4 a–d illustrate TEM of graphene quantum dots and GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, respectively. TEM images of the resulting GQDs show that these GQDs are uniform in size ranging from 20 to 30nm in diameter (Fig. 4a). According to the Fig. 4b, GQDs stacking to form large particles. Qin Li et al. report that this happens because at higher temperature most of tiny particles stack and form larger particles with inhomogeneous sizes [17]. According to the Fig. 4c and d, GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites have spherical shape with average size of 4–5nm. We believe that GQDs acts as capping agent and surrounding the initial TiO2 nucleation and suspend the growth of TiO2. GQD connected to the TiO2 by its CC, OH and NH groups that why there is not graphene sheet in the Fig. 4c and d. The optical properties of the as-synthesized GQDs, TiO2 and GQD/TiO2 nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 5 a–c. The ultraviolet-visible (UV/Vis) absorption spectrum (Fig. 5b) of the GQDs shows two significant excitonic absorption bands at about 250 and 350nm due to π-to-π* transition of CC and n-to-π* transition of CO groups, respectively [18]. Moreover, the existence of absorption band at ~270nm can be attributed to N-doped GQDs [19]. Fig. 5a presents the PL spectra of the as synthesized GQDs. The exact mechanism of PL of GQDs is not clear yet but it may happens because of electron-hole recombination, doping, quantum effect, free zig-zag sites with a carbene-like triplet ground state, edge structure, and surface defects in the functional groups of the GQDs [19]. As shown in Fig. 5a a broad emission centered at 450nm is observed in the emission spectrum (PL) with an excitation wavelength at 360nm (green line, Fig. 5a) and the excitation spectrum (PLE), reveals a broad peak centered at 365nm (pink line, Fig. 5a). To compare the optical properties of as synthesized GQD, TiO2 and GQD/TiO2 nanocomposites, the UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra prepared at Fig. 5b, c. As shown in Fig. 5b, TiO2 alone exhibits no absorption peak in the visible region of 400–800nm. With the incorporation of GQDs, the composites exhibit enhanced visible-light absorption [2]. As seen from the UV–Vis spectrum, the shape of peak of GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite and GQD was similar, while the absorption intensity in the range of 200–800nm was stronger than that of GQD and TiO2. The two possible mechanism to explain this enhancement is first, GQDs acts as sensitizer and second, charge transfer from GQD to TiO2. PL spectra (Fig. 5c) of GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibit lower emission intensity than that of bare TiO2and GQD which is preferable in case of employing the materials as catalysts in the photoreactions [2]. In the case of GQDs/TiO2 composite, the band edge of CB of TiO2 is lowered and the band edge of VB of GQDs is raised by forming Ti-O-C bond between GQD and TiO2 nanoparticles which lead to facilitate the injection of electron from GQDs to TiO2 and the drainage of hole from the TiO2 to GQDs. Such a process significantly reduces charge recombination and quench the PL of TiO2 and GQDs. In addition to PL quenching, we also observed a blue shift of PL spectra as indicated by the dash line in Fig. 5c. Fig. 6 schematically shows the possible mechanism of blue shift in PL. As seen from the Fig. 6, excited electron from GQDs transferred to CB of TiO2 and recombined with hole in V.B. of TiO2. Since energy gap of TiO2 is wider, emitted light shifted to shorter wavelength. The aqueous solution of GQDs exhibited light yellow color (Fig. 7a) in day light and bright blue and green emission under UV light (right inset in Fig. 7b and c), which could be easily observed with the naked eye. We figure out that it is possible to engineer the emission light and their color under UV light by adjust the temperature and reaction time (Fig. 8 ). As seen from Figs. 7 and 8, their colors could change from bright green to bright blue. The emission wavelength shifted to the higher wavelength by increasing time and temperature. Rhodamine B is an organic dye with ƛmx of 554nm that has been widely utilized in biochemical analysis, paper industry and cosmetic [20–24].

Photo-catalysis processes are similar to the water splitting processes. In both of them, photons are absorbed by an semiconductor and generate electron and holes. In the case of water splitting, generated electron reduced hydrogen ion (H+) and formed hydrogen molecules (H2) and electron oxidized water and formed O2. In the case of Photo-catalysis, generated electron and holes produced hydrogen and hydroxide radicals. In this research for the degradation of RhB, as-synthesized GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite was employed as the catalyst. 1mg/mL solution of synthesized GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite were added into 50mL RhB (10mg/L) solution. During the irradiation, 4mL solution was sampled and centrifuged to remove the catalysts and measure the concentration of RhB. Fig. 9 shows photocatalytic behavior of GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite under UV light. The result reveals that GQD/TiO2 nanocomposite showed much higher photocatalytic activity than GQD and TiO2nanoparticles.This probably happens because of the low recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes in GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite (as shown in PL spectra). About 88, 40 and 30% of RhB were degraded after 35min for GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite, TiO2 nanoparticles and GQD, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9, by using the GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite, RhB dye is nearly completely decolored after 80min by Uv light irradiation. For the pure TiO2 photocatalyst, however, only 53% RhB is degraded for the same irradiation time and GQDs alone also show low catalytic activity under the Uv light: About 45% RhB is degraded after 80min of irradiation. The degradation efficiency of pure GQDs can be ascribed to their large exciton binding energy (~0.8eV estimated for 2nm GQDs) that greatly increases the recombination rate of photoexcited charge carrier [2].

τ1/2 is a kinetic factor to evaluate the degradation rate. τ1/2 is 8 and 80 for GQDs/TiO2 composite and TiO2, respectively. It means the degradation rate for GQDs/TiO2 composite is 10 times of TiO2 which can be ascribed to the higher number of free e/h pairs in the composite. There are three possible reasons to explain the higher photocatalytic activity of composite compare to the TiO2. First, as mentioned previously, the electron-hole recombination rate in composite is lower than bare TiO2 (supported by PL results). Second, GQDs acts as sensitizer and inject electron to CB of TiO2 and composite absorbs whole range of spectrum from 200 to 800nm (supported by PL and UV–Vis results). Third, composite has higher surface area compare to the TiO2 (supported by SEM and TEM images).

GQDs and GQDs/TiO2 composite were successfully synthesized by hydrothermal and in situ sonochemistry method, respectively. Herein we used a novel and green precursor (corn powder) for synthesis of graphene quantum dots. The as-synthesized GQDs/TiO2 composite show much enhanced photocatalytic activity in the degradation of Rhodamin B compared to the bare TiO2 under the UV light irradiation. Base of the results, by using the GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite, RhB dye is nearly completely decolored after 80min. For the pure TiO2 photocatalyst, however, only 53% RhB is degraded for the same irradiation time and GQDs alone also show low catalytic activity under the Uv light: About 45% RhB is degraded after 80min of irradiation. It seems that GQDs suppressed the charge recombination of TiO2 and extended the absorption spectrum of TiO2 to the visible region. In other words, prepared nano-composite produced more hydrogen and hydroxide radicals which are necessary to degrade dye molecules. Possible mechanism for photo-degradation of nanocomposite is illustrated in Scheme 1 . In addition, GQDs acts as capping agents and reduces the size of as-prepared TiO2 nanoparticles.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/8990).
